title: Generate requirements for a compact dehumidifier for residen
stakeholder_needs: Generate requirements for a compact dehumidifier for residential use. It should remove at least 10 liters of moisture per day, operate below 45 dB, and meet EU energy-efficiency and safety requirements.
analysis: Stakeholders include end users (quiet operation, portability, intuitive controls), households (air comfort, mold prevention), compliance bodies (EU CE, safety, ecodesign/energy labeling), manufacturers (cost, supply chain, serviceability), and installers/retailers (setup simplicity, reliability, low returns). The given constraints set key measurable performance targets: capacity >=10 L/day and noise <45 dB. Requirements must cover functional (dehumidification modes, drainage), non-functional (acoustics, energy efficiency), regulatory (EU safety/EMC/RoHS/REACH, ecodesign/energy label where applicable), and verification (test conditions for L/day, dB measurement distance/standard, operating temperature/humidity range). Major risk is ambiguity in test conditions and which EU energy-efficiency framework applies to dehumidifiers (product category, metrics), plus environmental constraints (refrigerant rules) and user scenarios (room size, continuous drain).
clarification_questions: Under what standard test conditions should the 10 L/day capacity be verified (e.g., 27°C/60% RH or 30°C/80% RH), and what operating temperature/RH range is required?
What is the target room size/coverage and intended use case (single room portable vs whole-apartment), including preferred water removal method (tank only vs continuous drain/pump)?

Which specific EU compliance/labeling targets must be met (e.g., CE via LVD+EMC, RoHS/REACH, Ecodesign/energy label scheme if applicable), and are there constraints on refrigerant type (e.g., low-GWP R290) and maximum power consumption?

table.title: DFMEA - Compact Residential Dehumidifier (>=10 L/day, <45 dB, EU compliance)
rows: Provide safe energy storage and power output across operating temperature range
Fire, smoke, injury, property damage, product recall
Cell internal short, overcharge, mechanical damage, inadequate BMS limits, thermal insulation trapping heat
Cell supplier qualification, BMS OVP/UVP/OCP design review, pack mechanical protection, thermal modeling
Abuse testing (overcharge, crush), BMS fault-injection tests, pack-level UN38.3 pre-checks
Add redundant temperature sensors and independent hardware cutoff; increase creepage/clearance; add vent path away from user; strengthen pack enclosure
Concept DFMEA created; initial BMS schematic reviewed
Define clear battery abuse test matrix early; reuse validated BMS cutoff circuit from prior product
Only applicable if cordless SKU is pursued; otherwise mark N/A at program gate
Provide required airflow with low noise (<45 dB) and long life
Bearing wear leading to increased vibration/noise
Noise exceeds 45 dB, customer complaints/returns, reduced performance
Low-grade bearing, contamination ingress, imbalance, inadequate lubrication, high operating temperature
Bearing spec with L10 life margin, incoming inspection, rotor balancing spec, protective shroud
End-of-line vibration/noise test, HALT run-in sampling, teardowns
Upgrade to sealed bearings; add dust shield; tighten balance tolerance; implement 100% acoustic screening at rated mode
Current motor supplier data reviewed; no 100% acoustic screen in place
Set acoustic limits by operating mode and ambient; ensure measurement method matches spec (distance, room, background)
Critical to meet stakeholder requirement for quiet operation
Ensure unobstructed inlet airflow and user safety
Inlet grille allows finger access to rotating fan
Injury hazard, non-compliance with EU safety standards, CE failure
Grille opening too large, inadequate depth-to-blade, tolerance stack-up
Design to IEC/EN finger probe requirements, safety review, tolerance analysis
Finger-probe compliance test, dimensional inspection of grille and blade clearance
Reduce grille aperture and/or increase offset depth; add internal guard ring; update tolerance stack-up control plan
Initial grille concept not yet verified with probe tests
Lock safety-critical dimensions in drawings; add CTQ callouts for grille openings
Capture dust to protect heat exchanger and maintain airflow
Filter clogs quickly causing airflow reduction
Dehumidification capacity drops below 10 L/day, higher noise, icing risk, user dissatisfaction
Filter area too small, fine mesh too restrictive, no filter maintenance reminder, dusty environments
Filter sizing calculations, service interval definition, user manual instructions
Airflow sensor (if any) correlation tests, performance test at dust load, user trial feedback
Increase filter area; optimize mesh; add filter-clean reminder based on runtime; add easy-access design for cleaning
Prototype filter shows rapid pressure drop in bench tests
Define verification with standardized dust loading to reduce ambiguity; link to capacity requirement at RH/T conditions
Maintenance messaging important for low returns
Provide structural integrity, stability, and safe surface temperatures
Cracks at screw bosses leading to looseness/rattles
Increased noise, reduced perceived quality, potential exposure to live parts if severe
Boss design weak, over-torque, material creep, drop impact, poor gate location causing knit lines
Boss design guidelines, torque spec, material selection with impact modifier, drop-test targets
Drop test, torque audit, EOL rattle test, field return analysis
Add metal inserts or larger bosses; implement torque-limited drivers; update molding DFM for knit-line avoidance; add ribs
Initial boss geometry not reviewed for drop loads
Standardize fastener strategy across products; incorporate torque audit in production control plan
Noise target depends on rattle-free construction
Convert mains to safe low-voltage DC; meet EU safety/EMC
Overheating of power supply under high ambient
Burn hazard, fire risk, shutdown, non-compliance
Underspecified components, poor thermal design, blocked ventilation, high line voltage, counterfeit adapter
Use certified PSU (EN/IEC 62368-1), derating, supplier audits, thermal simulation
Thermal chamber test, PSU safety reports review, incoming authentication checks
Specify Class II certified PSU with temperature rise margin; add thermal fuse; enforce anti-counterfeit labeling and keyed connector
Supplier offers certified PSU but no anti-counterfeit plan yet
Treat PSU as safety-critical purchased part; keep traceability for CE technical file
If internal PSU, apply same controls at PCB/assembly level
Provide intuitive controls and clear status indications
UI misinterpreted (unclear icons/logic) causing wrong mode selection
User runs wrong humidity setpoint, perceived poor performance, higher noise, returns
Ambiguous labeling, inconsistent behavior, poor localization, lack of feedback on setpoint vs current RH
Usability review, icon standardization, localization process, UI state machine spec
Formative usability testing, HMI review checklist, software unit tests
Add clear humidity setpoint display; use standard symbols; add guided quick-start; implement long-press lock and confirmation tones (optional)
Early UI mock lacks setpoint/current RH clarity
Include user scenarios (night mode, continuous drain) in UI requirements and verification
Ensure any sound feedback can be disabled to support quiet use
Prevent water leakage from tank to housing
Gasket compression set leading to leak over time
Water damage to electronics, slip hazard, mold, customer complaints
Wrong elastomer, poor groove design, high clamp force variability, chemical exposure, aging
Material selection (EPDM/silicone), groove design standards, compression analysis
Leak test at EOL, accelerated aging + leak validation, incoming material certs
Redesign gland for correct squeeze; add secondary drip channel; specify compression set limit; add tank-in detection to stop operation on mis-seat
No accelerated aging data yet for gasket material
Leak paths must be controlled to protect safety and reduce returns; reuse proven gasket profiles where possible
Consider user handling cycles as key aging driver
Maintain airflow without whistling or tonal noise
Air duct resonance causes tonal noise >45 dB even at low speed
Fails noise requirement, poor user experience, negative reviews
Sharp edges, poor duct geometry, fan-blade pass frequency coupling, insufficient acoustic damping
CFD/acoustic simulation, rounded transitions, avoid cavity resonance dimensions
Anechoic/quiet-room noise spectrum test, design reviews against acoustic checklist
Add acoustic foam or resonator; adjust duct dimensions; implement fan speed dithering to reduce tonals; add edge radii
No spectral targets defined yet (tonal penalties)
Define measurement standard (e.g., ISO 3744/3745) and tonal evaluation method early to avoid ambiguity
Tonal noise often drives perceived loudness beyond dB(A)
Provide optional odor/particle reduction without excessive pressure drop
Reduced filtration effectiveness, dust fouling evaporator, warranty claims
Poor keying features, unclear orientation marks, loose fit tolerances
Poka-yoke design, clear markings, assembly instruction updates
Visual inspection, fit-check gauge, user manual verification
Add keyed frame; add positive latch; include orientation arrows; add QR video instructions
Current concept relies on friction fit only
Design for correct user installation to reduce returns; use common frame across SKUs
Optional accessory impacts core performance via airflow
Control fan/compressor speed efficiently and meet EMC
PWM switching noise causes EMC non-compliance / UI resets
CE EMC failure, product malfunction, returns
Poor PCB layout, insufficient filtering, inadequate grounding, cable coupling
EMC design rules, layout review, component selection (filters, ferrites), shield strategy
Pre-compliance EMC scans, conducted/radiated emissions tests, firmware watchdog tests
Add LC filters and common-mode chokes; revise ground planes; add shielding can; improve firmware brownout handling
Only conceptual PCB stack-up exists; no pre-scan scheduled
Schedule pre-compliance early; reuse known-good EMI filter network from prior CE products
Applies to entire appliance, not just motor drive
Charge termination failure leading to overcharge
Fire risk, cell damage, shortened life, CE safety failure
Firmware bug, wrong sense resistor, charger IC fault, inadequate redundancy
Use proven charger IC, redundant OVP hardware, code reviews, calibration procedure
Safety functional test, fault-injection, charger calibration verification
Add independent secondary OVP comparator; implement charge timeouts; add production calibration lock; add logging of charge faults
Charger firmware requirements not yet finalized
Never rely solely on software for safety cutoff; document safety architecture in technical file
Only applicable if internal charging exists
Maintain refrigerant containment for lifetime and meet F-gas expectations
Refrigerant leak at brazed joint or service port
Reduced dehumidification capacity (<10 L/day), increased energy use, environmental non-compliance, potential safety risk depending on refrigerant
Poor brazing process, vibration fatigue, material incompatibility, inadequate leak testing
Qualified brazing process, vibration isolation, component compatibility review, design for minimal joints
100% helium leak test, pressure decay test, end-of-line performance test correlation
Increase joint robustness; add vibration dampers; implement stricter leak rate limits; add process monitoring for brazing ताप/time
Leak limit not yet allocated to system-level capacity retention
Allocate leak-rate budget to meet capacity over life; ensure refrigerant selection aligns with EU rules and safety class
Verification must specify ambient T/RH for capacity claims
Direct condensate to tank/drain without internal pooling
Internal condensate drip onto PCB due to splash or misrouting
Electrical short, corrosion, intermittent faults, safety risk
Poor drip tray geometry, inadequate splash guards, tank removal during operation, tilt handling
Condensate routing design review, IPX splash considerations, conformal coating policy
Water ingress test, tilted-operation test, EOL functional soak test
Add drip shields and gutters; relocate PCB away from condensate path; add tank-removed interlock to stop compressor; consider conformal coat on critical areas
PCB placement overlaps condensate path in current layout
Design must consider real user actions (emptying tank while running); add to verification plan
Critical for low returns and CE safety evidence
